#+TITLE: Quiet code as personal craft
#+SUBTITLE: Technical artifacts that stay subordinate to the essay
#+AUTHOR: Hubert Behaghel
#+DATE: 2026-05-10
#+LANGUAGE: en
#+LATEX_CLASS: hub-article
#+EXPORT_EYEBROW: Personal systems

#+begin_standfirst
Technical material should feel composed and precise without pulling the page away
from the essay voice.
#+end_standfirst

* Reading a small artifact

The article should be able to mention a ~cache-key~ inline, make *bold
emphasis*, add an /italic aside/, retire a +struck-through draft+, and point to
a [[https://example.com/personal-systems][related note]] without leaving the essay
voice. It should keep the reader in prose before the technical surface appears.

The ordinary reading list stays native:

- Unordered reader note with a quiet rhythm.
- Another bullet that checks vertical spacing against the prose.

The operating sequence stays native too:

1. First numbered checkpoint.
2. Second numbered checkpoint.

The terms should remain useful without becoming a glossary panel:

- Term marker :: Definition list copy that explains one compact idea.
- Quiet artifact :: A technical detail presented as part of the essay.

#+begin_src emacs-lisp
(defun hub/article-cache-key (slug locale)
  "Return a stable cache key for SLUG and LOCALE."
  (format "%s:%s" locale slug))
#+end_src

#+begin_callout
The callout-like prose should behave as a quiet aside: useful enough to notice,
but visually below the argument and far away from campaign chrome.
#+end_callout

#+begin_quote
Technical artifacts are furniture for thought, not a second interface competing
with the essay. A longer quoted thought should keep its left rule for every
line it occupies, including the line that only appears after wrapping.
#+end_quote

#+CAPTION: Small technical decisions should stay legible inside the article measure.
| Artifact | Personal treatment |
|----------+--------------------|
| Inline code | restrained mono phrase |
| Source block | quiet prose-level sample |
| Table | calm evidence rhythm |

The closing prose confirms that source blocks, inline code, tables, quotes, and
callouts survive the personal article export path without inheriting Veriff or
campaign-style layout behavior.
